body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

transcript.text: all right tick tock it's the bottom of the hour find a seat put your conversation on pause for a minute let's have a meeting um this is the routing area open meeting thank you for being here um I'm John Jim G Andrew and there's Andrew on TV um we have the same note well as everybody else does um and hopefully you've already noted it well um so we have a short meeting um if somebody is willing to take notes um that would be awesome um thank you so much Darren um you know we don't need extensive ones action items the usual um agenda bashing hopefully there's not a bash but you know if there's anything that we're missing on here please speak up um otherwise we'll do a very brief state of the area and then we've asked three work groups chosen not at random to report out um by the way at any one of these things if you happen to feel like you you know want to report out and we haven't asked you to you're always allowed to propose a um a talk so um I think there's been less action than there was in the last couple meetings but uh raw has closed we have been talking that up so that should not be a surprise to anyone it's rolled into Deb net um Babel is expecting to close soon and we don't currently have any new working groups or bosss um so thanks so much to Andrew Alon and welcome to Gunter and uh so this is the the sort of tldr of working group assignment changes but that's the full list of how the uh the working groups are going to be distributed um so everything stays the same except Gunter is taking over with LSR I'm taking over with IDR um you can read the rest um and that's the full list yeah that's it for our uh for our slides so and get the slides queed up for first uh Jeffrey soon as I can remember how to operate the slide clicky thing okay so I'm Jeffrey Dr uh a co-chair for the rift working group um there's another Jeff um so Jeff and Jeffrey um so Rift is a relatively new uh routing protocol for data center um it's basically link routing in the Northbound and distance Vector routing in the southbound the working group was chartered with three initial deliverables base protocol specification applicability statement and the Y model uh finally we finished the the Riff Bas based protocol the draft Rift Rift D20 pass ITF last call and thanks for Jim and Alvaro going through the painful process of reviewing it and lot of up revisions and also of course the the co-authors of the protocol and the working group members um the applicability statement needs an a re one revision to catch up the changes in the most recent change uh face spec change and the Y model drafts uh it's in the ad value valuation or ad followup but we I we believe we also need one previs uh to to catch to catch up so that's the existing work um there are quite some followable work that we want to do um we have not started a rechar process but we are discussing uh uh new Charter and here is some basic uh items we thought about want to continue to work on key value store policy guided prefix segment routing multicast uh leering topology and uh dragonfly topologies and also using Rift zero touch provisioning for automatic provisioning of other protocols or mechanisms for example us it for evpn overlay or ISS flood reflection topology Discovery things like that also we would like to explore the use and extensions of the reef protocol for the newer AIML data center architectures so that's the thing we are thinking of obviously this is we need more discussions and to finish all these things um so that's the update from the working uh R for working group uh thank you and um well Jim yeah I just wanted to say so um so the the base spec has as you said has gone through last call um I do encourage people to go and look at the document the more feedback I get um before it goes through the final process the better um I did want to say thank you to um a couple of people Donald um very kindly did a very thorough review on thankful for that um and also both of my current coads both um Andrew and and John have uh given it a good once over as well so so I do encourage people to go and look at the document and um and give us as much feedback it's it's heavy going because it's a big document um but there's some really pretty cool stuff in there um if you haven't already read it so any feedback uh definitely appreciated what's that oh Andrew Andrew were you wanting to say something your mic looks like it's hot mic should been off okay my mistake hi um presenting on behalf of my co-chair richy who is not here today so what's L um just this brief refresher uh list is an overlay routing that separates the identifier and the locator so we have actually a maping encap dcap protocol the map server is actually responsible for the mapping of the Eid which is the identifiers in to the route locators which are typically the IP addresses while the scope of L technology has been applicable to a much long larger span since where it starts started that we will see in the future next slides and the big news is that the working group has a new Charter and was approve January 25th so I am just going to show the Charter work items but I'm not going to go through them in detail here just for the sake of time so we're moving some of the standard some of our previous experimental documents to standard track but there's still a few of them that we need to do and they are listed here for example the multicast the can call addresses um then we will be working on a reliable transport for the mapping system just because there are certain cases for skill it doesn't work very well there's a lot of retransmission and definitely a reliable service would be better and um the Yang models of course uh will be exploring the traffic engineering that has been several propositions over the years but we we want to finish on those and um that transversal is something which is we will see almost ready uh privacy and security we have been working on other r ing um eids and um having encrypted uh identifiers as well so we'll be diving into this then um let's external connectivity is more about list talking to other services that are non list sites and U of course Mobility U one big advantage of list is that because there is a separation between the identifier and the locator and a mapping system Mobility works well for it and um last is we'll have much more applications and less that we did previously one of them uh for example is automobile Industries so we're still having um we're actually going to revamp the use case uh doc now just update on the documents that we have here um most of these docks um that we have are based on the charter and the priority in the charter so the first one is um the one with the Red Dot is uh traffic engineering is actually needs some review right now we're almost done with the name and coding um and um the only reason why it's still here is because we're are discussing now whether we want to put it into a standard track rather than experimental track the next one the nexton is flag this red here but actually the story on that one is the review was done on a very older in an older version so I think it's ready to get resubmitted so we'll do that and then um there's two other drafts right now reliable transport and Yang Yang mod is closer to the end U there's a couple of questions that came up and that the working group is getting f on what we want to do for example some of the coordinates are done in Latitude rather than in uh decimal references for example and um the last one the reliable transport this went FL review by the che chairs and uh we want to have some feedback they they're still going to address our feedback all right and then the next so we have a few you know other rfcs here who have to um or drafts that have to go into uh again that cycle so right now one of those is the net transversal uh it is um I think pretty close as well so the others will come back um as we look at this priority which is basically the M student that we're trying to aim at so most of the graphs that we've seen just before can be actually grouped into those into those milestones and that's where we are aiming for for this year and next year hopefully getting all these papers out and and then we'll reevaluate hi I'm Tony Lee I'm presenting on behalf of TK Nick and our outgoing chair Adrian uh so as you may recall Loa stepped down after serving his chair for 20 years uh we'd like to again thank him for his service um I think we should be saying that for another 20 years um Adrienne stepped up to fill his role and has been helping things move along and he one of his preconditions was that he stepped down after this ITF uh so he will be stepping down and I am now the incoming co-chair we have lots of documents in progress we are juggling many many things I am not going to read all of them in detail uh if you would like to talk about them please come to the working group we will be happy to talk about them then uh the most important thing to know is that we are still very much working on m& network actions and we have lots of documents in the queue for that and those we hope to progress very rapidly at this point so lots of little things things completing working group last call things coming out things cued for publication again we're working on m&a um we have uh some reports of implementation experience um not all of it good but that's interesting we're work we're working through that um trying to figure out what to do about that if anything uh we want to have an interim after 119 to help settle this so we'll continue still doing some house CL cleaning for some expired a IDs um just got large backlog of documents uh we had an interesting Leon statement from itu sg11 uh this actually didn't come to us directly uh but it bounced off to MLS and we've responded this is about doing basically trade route for MLS and basically sent sg11 a diplomatically worded note all right uh that's all my slides any questions all right thank you that went really fast um the the the one thing that occurred to me that we didn't have on the slides up front was we have routing Area Office hours um on uh Thursday at lunchtime so you know please feel free to Avail yourself of that um we also apparently have an extra 44 minutes right now so um unless anybody has anything else they'd like to bring up right now we are concluded but you know if you there's anything you want to talk about step right up and I do see Andrew at the mic so please go ahead Andrew yeah I just wanted to say a sincere thank you to John and Jim um for their time working with me it's been great guys and all your hard work during the time has been appreciated and you will both be missed as I step down so thank you and if everybody can give them a round of applause for all the hard work they do that would be good I'm tearing up man we'll miss you too don't be a stranger and uh with that we are in fact concluded thank you everybody that was quick yeah yeah uh oh look at look out look out John incoming to figure out to times available if you guys

transcript.text:
SPEAKER S1: Tick tock. It's the bottom of the hour. Find a seat. Put your conversation on. Pause for a minute. Let's have a meeting. Um, this is the routing area. Open meeting. Thank you for being here. Um, I'm John Jim Gunther. Gunther. Andrew. And there's Andrew on TV. Um, we have the same note. Well, as everybody else does. Um. And hopefully you've already noted it. Well, um. So we have a short meeting. Um, if somebody is willing to take notes. Um. That would be awesome. Um, thank you so much, Darren. Um, you know, we don't need extensive ones, action items, the usual, um, agenda bashing. Hopefully there's not a bash, but, you know, if there's anything that we're missing on here, please speak up. Um, otherwise, we'll do a very brief state of the area. And then we've asked three working groups chosen, not at random to report out, um, by the way, at any one of these things, if you happen to feel like you, you know, want to report out and we haven't asked you to, you're always allowed to propose a, um, a talk. So, um, I think there's been less action than there was in the last couple meetings, but Raw has closed. We have been talking that up, so that should not be a surprise to anyone. It's rolled into debt net. Um, Babel is expecting to close soon, and we don't currently have any new working groups or boffs. Um, so thanks so much to Andrew Alston and welcome to Gunter. And uh, so this is the the sort of tldr of working group assignment changes, but that's the full list of how the, uh, the working groups are going to be distributed. Um, so everything stays the same except Gunter is taking over with LSR. I'm taking over with IDR. Um, you can read the rest. Um.
SPEAKER S2: And that's the full list?
SPEAKER S1: Yeah, that's it for our, uh, for our slides. So let me get the slides queued up for first. Uh, Geoffrey. As soon as I can remember how to operate the slide clicky thing.
SPEAKER S3: Okay, so I'm Jeffrey Zhang, uh, co-chair for the working group. Um, there's another Jeff. Um, so Jeff and Jeffrey. Um, so rift is a relatively new, uh, routing protocol for data center. Um, it's basically a link state routing in the northbound and distance vector routing in the southbound. The working group was chartered with three initial deliverables base Protocol specification, applicability statement, and the young model. Um, finally, we finished the the base protocol, the draft Rift rift Dash 20 past IETF last call. Thank you. And thanks for for Jim and Alvaro, uh, going through the painful process of reviewing it and a lot of revisions and also, of course, the the co-authors of the protocol and the working group members, um, the applicability statement, this, uh, one revision to catch up, the changes in the most recent, uh, spec change and the young model drafts, uh, it's in the ad evaluation or ad follow up, but we, I, we believe we also need one revision, uh, to, to catch, to catch up. So that's the existing work. Um. There are quite some follow up work that we want to do. Um, we have not started a recharter process, but we are discussing, uh, new charter and here's some basic, uh, items we, we thought about we want to continue to work on key value store policy, guided prefix, segment routing, multicast and different topology and dragonfly topologies, and also using rift zero touch provisioning for automatic provisioning of other protocols and mechanisms. For example, use it for evpn overlay or ISIS, flood reflection, topology discovery, things like that. Also, we would like to explore the use and extensions of the rift protocol for the newer AIML data center architectures. So that's the thing we are thinking of. Obviously this is we need more discussions and to finish all these things. Um, so that's the update from the working uh, working group. Yeah.
SPEAKER S2: Cool.
SPEAKER S1: Um, thank you. And, um, well, Jim.
SPEAKER S2: Yeah, I just wanted to say so. Um, so the the base spec is, as you said, has gone through last call. Um, I do encourage people to go and look at the document. The more feedback I get, um, before it goes through the final process, the better. Um, I did want to say thank you to, um, a couple of people. Donald, um, very kindly did a very thorough review. I'm very thankful for that. Um, and also, both of my current codes, both, um, Andrew and John have, uh, given it a good once over as well. So, so I do encourage people to go and look at the document and, um, and give us as much feedback. It's, it's heavy going because it's a big document. Um, but there's some really pretty cool stuff in there, um, if you haven't already read it. So any feedback? Uh, definitely appreciated. Andrew's okay. Thanks. What's that? Oh, Andrew.
SPEAKER S1: Andrew, are you wanting to say something? You. Mike. Looks like it's hot.
SPEAKER S4: Um, much should have been off.
SPEAKER S1: Okay. My mistake. And.
SPEAKER S5: Here I, um, presenting on behalf of my co-chair, who is not here today. So what's up? Um, just a brief refresher. Uh, this is an overlay routing that separates the identifier and the locator. So we have actually a mapping encap Decap protocol. The metaphor is actually responsible for the mapping of the id, which is the input identifiers in our list to the root locators, which are typically the IP addresses. Well, the scope of list technologies has been applicable to a much longer, larger span since what it starts. Started out what we'll see in the future. Next slides. And the big news is that the working group has a new charter and was approved January 25th. So I am just going to show the charter work items, but I'm not going to go through them in detail here just for the sake of time. So we're moving some of the standard, some of our previous experimental documents to standard track, but there's still a few of them that we need to do, and they are listed here. For example, the multicast, the canonical addresses. Then we will be working on a reliable transport for the mapping system. Just because there are certain cases for scalability doesn't work very well. There's a lot of retransmission and definitely a reliable service would be better. And, um, the young models, of course, uh, will be exploring the traffic engineering that has been several propositions over the years, but we we want to finish on those. And um, not transversal is something which is what we'll see. Almost ready. Um, privacy and security. We have been working on advertising, um, IEDs and, um, having encrypted, uh, identifiers as well. So we'll be diving into this then. Um, let's external connectivity is more about less talking to other services that are non Lisp sites and uh, of course mobility. Uh, one big advantage of this is that because there is a separation between the identifier and the locator and the mapping system, um, mobility works well for it. And, um, last is we'll have much more applications and less than we did previously. One of them, for example, is automobile industries. So we're still having um, we're actually going to revamp the use case. Uh, doc, now, just an update on the documents that we have here. Um, most of these docs, um, that we have are based on the charter and the priority in the charter. So the first one is, um, the one with the red dot is, uh, traffic engineering is actually, uh, needs some review right now. We're almost done with the name encoding. Um, and, um, the only reason why it's still here is because we are discussing now whether we want to put it into a standards track rather than an experimental track. The next one, the next one is flat, as read here, but actually the story on that one is the review was done on a very older and an older version, so I think it's ready to get resubmitted. So we'll do that. And then um, there's two other drafts right now, Reliable Transport and General Yamato is closer to the end. Um, there's a couple of questions that came up and that the working group is getting called on what we want to do. For example, some of the coordinates are done in latitude rather than in, uh, decimal references, for example. And um, the last one, the reliable transport. This was reviewed by the two chairs. And uh, we want to have some feedback. They're still going to address our feedback. All right. And then the next cell we have if you are, you know, other RFCs here who have to um, or drafts that have to go into. Again that cycle. So right now one of those is the transversal. Uh, it is um, I think pretty close as well. So the others will come back. Um, as we look at this priority, which is basically the milestone that we are trying to aim at. So most of the graphs that we've seen just before, or can be actually grouped into those, into those milestones, and that's where we are aiming for for this year and next year, hopefully getting all these papers out and and then we'll reevaluate.
SPEAKER S2: Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: Thank you.
SPEAKER S6: Thank you.
SPEAKER S1: Hi, I'm Tony Lee.
SPEAKER S7: I'm presenting on behalf of Tarek, Nick and our outgoing chair, Adrian. Uh, so, as you may recall, Laura stepped down after serving as chair for 20 years. Uh, we'd like to again thank him for his service. Um, I think we should be saying that for another 20 years. Um, Adrian stepped up to fill his role and has been helping things move along, and he. One of his preconditions was that he stepped down after this ETF. Uh, so he will be stepping down and I am now the incoming co-chair. We have lots of documents in progress. We are juggling many, many things. I am not going to read all of them in detail. Uh, if you would like to talk about them, please come to the working group. We will be happy to talk about them then. Uh, the most important thing to know is that we are still very much working on M&A network actions, and we have lots of documents in the queue for that, and those we hope to progress very rapidly at this point. So lots of little things. Things completing working group last call things coming out. Things queued for publication. Uh, again we're working on M&A.
